claims.claim_text: The Black Friday e-commerce outage anecdote is presented as a real 2021 incident with specific capacity, duration, and loss figures.
claims.anchor_text: In late 2021, an e-commerce startup launched their highly anticipated Black Friday sale. They had built their application on large EC2 instances and anticipated heavy traffic, so they provisioned twenty massive servers manually the night before. However, the traffic surge was three times larger than expected. The servers hit 100% CPU utilization within minutes. By the time the engineering team logged in, spun up new instances manually, installed the application dependencies, and registered them with the load balancer, two hours had passed. The website was unresponsive, carts were abandoned, and the startup lost an estimated two million dollars in sales.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A team that manually provisions EC2 capacity ahead of a major traffic event can still be overwhelmed if demand exceeds forecasts and new instances take too long to bring online.
claims.rationale: This is an unattributed incident narrative with precise operational and financial details.

claims.claim_text: AWS offers hundreds of instance types.
claims.anchor_text: AWS offers hundreds of instance types optimized to fit different use cases.
claims.suggested_rewrite: AWS offers many instance types optimized for different use cases.
claims.rationale: The exact count changes over time and the sentence does not need a drifting numeric claim.

claims.claim_text: EC2 groups instances into general purpose, compute optimized, memory optimized, storage optimized, and accelerated computing families.
claims.rationale: AWS instance-type documentation explicitly defines these categories and their intended workload profiles.

claims.claim_text: T-series instances are burstable and use CPU credits that accrue when utilization is below baseline and are spent during bursts.
claims.rationale: AWS burstable-instance documentation directly explains CPU credit accrual and spending.

claims.claim_text: Instance names such as `m6i.xlarge` encode the family/series, generation, options or attributes, and size.
claims.rationale: AWS documents the instance naming convention and each component of the identifier.

claims.claim_text: The comparison table's hourly price figures are time-bound example prices.
claims.anchor_text: Prices shown are approximate On-Demand hourly rates in `us-east-1` as of 2025 and will vary over time.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The table below uses representative `us-east-1` Linux On-Demand prices for a few common instance types; always verify current pricing before making production cost decisions.
claims.rationale: The teaching point is useful, but exact hourly prices are time-sensitive and should be framed as illustrative.

claims.claim_text: The module's recommendation that M-series 'usually make more sense' than T-series for production workloads is an uncited design judgment.
claims.anchor_text: For production workloads that need reliable performance, General Purpose M-series instances usually make more sense than burstable T-series, despite the slightly higher hourly cost.
claims.suggested_rewrite: For production workloads that need reliable, steady CPU performance, fixed-performance instance families are often a better fit than burstable T-series instances.
claims.rationale: The operational guidance is reasonable, but the original wording states a broad preference too categorically.

claims.claim_text: Graviton instances generally offer 20-40% better price-performance than Intel equivalents.
claims.anchor_text: They generally offer 20-40% better price-performance ratios compared to their Intel equivalents.
claims.suggested_rewrite: They can often offer better price-performance than comparable x86-based instances, depending on workload, generation, and region.
claims.rationale: The exact percentage varies by instance generation and workload, but the broader lesson is well-supported.

claims.claim_text: On-Demand EC2 pricing is pay-as-you-go with no long-term commitment and Linux usage is billed per second with a 60-second minimum.
claims.rationale: AWS's On-Demand pricing page directly states the commitment model and billing behavior.

claims.claim_text: Standard Reserved Instances can be sold in the Reserved Instance Marketplace, while Convertible RIs cannot.
claims.rationale: AWS's RI documentation explicitly distinguishes marketplace eligibility for Standard versus Convertible RIs.

claims.claim_text: Savings Plans are measured as a dollars-per-hour commitment for a 1- or 3-year term, and Compute Savings Plans can apply across families, sizes, Regions, OSes, and tenancy.
claims.rationale: AWS Savings Plans documentation directly describes the commitment model and flexibility scope.

claims.claim_text: Spot Instances can be interrupted and typically provide a two-minute interruption notice.
claims.rationale: AWS Spot interruption documentation directly states the two-minute notice behavior.

claims.claim_text: Dedicated Hosts are physical EC2 servers dedicated to one customer and are useful for server-bound licensing or hardware-isolation requirements.
claims.rationale: AWS's EC2 pricing page directly describes Dedicated Hosts as fully dedicated physical servers for your use.

claims.claim_text: The annual cost example for a continuously running `m6i.xlarge` uses precise yearly totals and discount assumptions that should be illustrative rather than fixed.
claims.anchor_text: **Cost example**: A single `m6i.xlarge` running 24/7 for a year at On-Demand rates costs approximately $0.192/hr x 8,760 hrs = **$1,682/year**. With a 3-year Savings Plan (all upfront), that same compute drops to roughly **$672/year**—a 60% savings. With Spot pricing (assuming ~70% average discount), an equivalent workload costs roughly **$504/year**, but you must design for interruptions.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Cost example**: A continuously running `m6i.xlarge` can cost well over a thousand dollars per year on On-Demand pricing, while commitment-based discounts and Spot pricing can lower costs substantially depending on the workload design and plan you choose.
claims.rationale: The underlying lesson is valid, but the exact annual totals depend on current prices and purchase-option assumptions.

claims.claim_text: The gp3 bullet uses outdated maximum performance numbers for provisioned IOPS and throughput.
claims.anchor_text: *   **gp3 (General Purpose SSD)**: The default for most workloads. Provides a baseline of 3,000 IOPS and 125 MiB/s throughput, with the ability to provision up to 16,000 IOPS and 1,000 MiB/s independently of storage capacity. This decoupling is a major improvement over gp2, which tied IOPS directly to volume size.
claims.suggested_rewrite: *   **gp3 (General Purpose SSD)**: The default for most workloads. It includes baseline IOPS and throughput, and lets you provision additional performance independently of storage capacity. This decoupling is a major improvement over gp2, which tied IOPS directly to volume size.
claims.rationale: Current AWS EBS documentation no longer matches the specific maximum values stated here.

claims.claim_text: EBS snapshots are point-in-time backups stored in Amazon S3 and subsequent snapshots are incremental.
claims.rationale: AWS snapshot documentation directly states that EBS snapshots are point-in-time, incremental backups stored in S3.

claims.claim_text: Fast Snapshot Restore creates volumes that are fully initialized at creation and removes first-access latency penalties.
claims.rationale: AWS FSR documentation directly describes these behaviors.

claims.claim_text: On Linux instances, EC2 user data can run shell scripts or cloud-init directives, runs as root, runs only on first boot by default, and writes output to `/var/log/cloud-init-output.log`.
claims.rationale: The EC2 user-data guide directly covers these execution and debugging details.

claims.claim_text: The module says most production teams adopt the hybrid AMI-plus-User-Data pattern.
claims.span_hint: section: Golden Image vs. User Data
claims.anchor_text: The hybrid approach is what most production teams adopt.
claims.suggested_rewrite: A hybrid approach is a common production pattern because it balances fast boot times with runtime flexibility.
claims.rationale: This is a broad market-frequency claim without an attributable source.

claims.claim_text: Application Load Balancers operate at Layer 7, distribute traffic across targets in multiple Availability Zones, and route traffic only to healthy targets.
claims.rationale: AWS ALB documentation directly describes the OSI layer, multi-AZ targeting, and health-based routing.

claims.claim_text: ALB target-group health checks let you configure path, interval, timeout, and healthy or unhealthy thresholds.
claims.rationale: The target-group health-check guide documents these exact settings.

claims.claim_text: ALB listener rules support host-based and path-based routing.
claims.rationale: AWS listener-rule condition documentation explicitly covers host-header and path-pattern routing.

claims.claim_text: ALB target groups support sticky sessions, and the default deregistration delay is 300 seconds.
claims.rationale: AWS target-group attribute documentation directly states both capabilities.

claims.claim_text: Auto Scaling groups maintain desired capacity and replace unhealthy instances to self-heal.
claims.rationale: AWS Auto Scaling health-check documentation directly describes replacement behavior for unhealthy instances.

claims.claim_text: The cooldown paragraph mixes unsupported defaults with workload-specific tuning advice.
claims.anchor_text: **Cooldown periods matter**: The `ScaleOutCooldown` (default 300s) prevents the ASG from launching a storm of new instances before the previous batch has had time to warm up and reduce load. Setting it too low causes thrashing; setting it too high causes sluggish response. For web apps behind an ALB, 60-120 seconds for scale-out and 300 seconds for scale-in is a reasonable starting point.
claims.suggested_rewrite: **Warmup and scaling timing matter**: if you scale too aggressively you can thrash, and if you scale too slowly you can respond sluggishly. Tune warmup and scaling timing to match how long your instances actually take to become useful.
claims.rationale: The specific defaults and recommended timings are not generally supportable as written and depend on the scaling method and workload.

claims.claim_text: The EC2 metadata service is available at `169.254.169.254`, IMDSv2 uses session tokens, and setting `http-tokens required` disables IMDSv1.
claims.rationale: AWS's IMDS documentation directly covers the endpoint, token flow, and enforcement behavior.

claims.claim_text: The security note ties IMDSv1 and SSRF to the 2019 Capital One breach via a misconfigured WAF and stolen IAM role credentials.
claims.rationale: The DOJ complaint is the primary source for the breach mechanics, but `justice.gov` is off the trusted allowlist.

claims.claim_text: EC2 Mac instances run on Mac mini hardware integrated with the AWS Nitro System and require a minimum 24-hour Dedicated Host allocation.
claims.rationale: AWS's launch announcement directly states the Mac mini/Nitro integration and the 24-hour minimum host allocation.

claims.claim_text: As of February 2024, AWS charges $0.005 per hour for public IPv4 addresses, including attached in-use addresses.
claims.rationale: AWS's announcement directly states the February 1, 2024 pricing change and rate.

claims.claim_text: The Nitro bullet mixes a supported high-level description with an unsupported specific 5-10% pre-Nitro overhead figure.
claims.anchor_text: The AWS Nitro System is a combination of dedicated hardware and a lightweight hypervisor. It offloads network, storage, and security functions to dedicated custom chips, delivering nearly all of the server's compute and memory resources directly to your instances, eliminating traditional hypervisor overhead. Pre-Nitro instances (like m4, c4) lost 5-10% of resources to the hypervisor.
claims.suggested_rewrite: The AWS Nitro System is a combination of dedicated hardware and a lightweight hypervisor. It offloads networking, storage, and security functions, reducing virtualization overhead and leaving more host resources available to instances.
claims.rationale: The broad Nitro architecture claim is supportable, but the specific historical percentage is not well-backed here.

claims.claim_text: A single Auto Scaling group can use mixed instance types and mixed On-Demand/Spot purchasing options, which requires a mixed instances policy backed by launch templates.
claims.rationale: AWS mixed-instances-policy documentation directly supports this capability and its launch-template requirement.

further_reading.title: Amazon EC2 Instance Type Specifications
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the best primary reference for family categories, instance capabilities, and workload fit.

further_reading.title: Health Checks for Instances in an Auto Scaling Group
further_reading.why_relevant: This explains the self-healing behavior that underpins the module's ASG design patterns.

further_reading.title: Run Commands When You Launch an EC2 Instance with User Data Input
further_reading.why_relevant: This is the primary guide for User Data, cloud-init behavior, and debugging bootstrapping failures.

further_reading.title: Use the Instance Metadata Service to Access Instance Metadata
further_reading.why_relevant: This covers IMDSv2 token flow, enforcement, and the safest metadata-access patterns for EC2.

notes: Quiz items, hands-on exercise steps, and code-block-only assertions were intentionally excluded per task rules. Pricing examples were treated conservatively where figures are illustrative or time-bound.
